featured.title: Weekend in Berlin: A 48-Hour Itinerary That Fits Your Arrival
featured.summary: A realistic 48-hour Berlin itinerary for Friday-evening or Saturday-morning arrivals, with one central Saturday line, a slower Sunday and no cross-city race.
featured.alt: Museum Island and the Berlin TV Tower beside the River Spree
featured.label: Newest weekend plan

miniPosts.title: 2 Days in Berlin: A Realistic Itinerary for First-Time Visitors
miniPosts.summary: Two days in Berlin work when the geography stays simple. Follow a realistic first-day route through the historic centre, then use the second day for the Berlin Wall Memorial, East Side Gallery and Kreuzberg.
miniPosts.alt: Museum Island and the Berlin TV Tower seen across the Spree

miniPosts.title: Berlin Natural History Museum: The World's Tallest Dinosaur, and How to Visit (2026)
miniPosts.summary: The Museum für Naturkunde, Berlin's natural history museum, holds the tallest mounted dinosaur skeleton on Earth, a real Tyrannosaurus rex, and the priceless Berlin Archaeopteryx. Here are 2026 tickets and hours, the exhibits worth your time, how to get there, and an honest take on whether it is worth it.
miniPosts.alt: The glass-roofed central hall of the Berlin Natural History Museum, with the towering Giraffatitan dinosaur skeleton on the left and a Diplodocus alongside, visitors standing at their feet for scale

miniPosts.title: German Numbers for Tourists: How to Read Berlin Prices, Platforms and Menus (2026)
miniPosts.summary: German numbers come at you fast in Berlin: a price at a bakery, a platform called out at Hauptbahnhof, the deposit on a bottle. This guide covers the one rule that makes them click (the small digit comes first), how prices and the comma work, what Gleis and the line numbers mean, and an interactive tool to build and hear any German number.
miniPosts.alt: Euro banknotes showing the numbers 20 and 50, the printed figures visitors handle every day in Berlin

miniPosts.title: Berlin Trams: The Tourist Guide to the M-Lines Nobody Explains
miniPosts.summary: Berlin trams confuse visitors: they only run in the old East, carry letters instead of numbers, and nobody hands you the rules. Here is why the network stops at the old border, the 2026 ticket you already have, and the handful of M-lines actually worth riding.
miniPosts.alt: A yellow Berlin tram on line M4 stopped at Alexanderplatz with the city behind it

posts.title: Where to Watch the 2026 World Cup in Berlin (No, There's No Brandenburg Gate Fan Mile This Year)
posts.summary: The 2026 World Cup is in North America, and Berlin has no Brandenburg Gate fan mile this year. Here is where to actually watch: free screens, beer gardens and bars, with every kick-off in Berlin time.
posts.alt: A summer evening football public viewing in Berlin with a relaxed crowd, outdoor screen and TV Tower skyline

posts.title: Currency Exchange in Berlin: Where to Change Money (and Where Not To) in 2026
posts.summary: Most visitors do not need to exchange cash in Berlin at all. A local guide to getting euros without the hidden margin: bank ATMs, the airport-desk trap, the dynamic-conversion question, and how much cash to actually carry.
posts.alt: Close-up of euro banknotes, a fifty and a twenty, the cash most visitors think they need to exchange before arriving in Berlin

posts.title: Hidden Places in Central Berlin: Anhalter Bahnhof to Alexanderplatz
posts.summary: Central Berlin hides some of its strongest history in plain sight. Follow one 5.3 km walk from Anhalter Bahnhof to Alexanderplatz through a ruined station, a GDR watchtower, Tränenpalast, memorials and old railway architecture.
posts.alt: The surviving brick portal of Anhalter Bahnhof at Askanischer Platz in Berlin.
